context: Darius says, I know that the Lord that you serve continually, I know He is able to deliver thee.
point_summary: Daniel's peaceful sleep in the lions' den illustrates the same sustaining peace David describes in Psalm 3, showing that trust left with God produces rest even amid mortal danger.

context: Daniel, is your God whom you serve continually able to deliver thee?
point_summary: Daniel's ability to sleep peacefully in the face of the lions' den illustrates the same sustaining peace David describes in Psalm 3—both men rested because they entrusted their circumstances to God.

context: Darius, he can't even sleep, right? I mean, he's all kinds of messed up because Daniel has to go into the den of lions.
point_summary: Daniel's deliverance from the lions' den illustrates the Hebrews 11 phrase "stopped the mouths of lions," with Darius's sleepless night and morning question affirming that God continually delivers those who serve Him.

context: And he says, is your God that you serve continually able to deliver thee?
point_summary: Daniel's deliverance from the lions' den illustrates the Hebrews 11 phrase "stopped the mouths of lions," with Darius's anxious question to Daniel confirming that God is able to deliver those who serve Him continually.

context: if it be so, if you're going to send us into this fiery furnace, we want you to understand that our God whom we serve is able to deliver us from the burning fiery furnace, and He will deliver us out of thine hand, O King.
point_summary: The three Hebrew men's declaration that God "is able to deliver" affirms an unwavering confidence that divine deliverance is certain, whether it comes from, through, or in the midst of the fiery trial.

context: But it says this, if not, v. 18, be it known unto thee, O King, that we will not serve thy gods nor worship the golden image which thou hast set up.
point_summary: Even if deliverance means death, the three Hebrews' refusal to bow demonstrates an unconditional faithfulness to God that transcends the outcome of any earthly trial.

context: We wouldn't read Daniel 3 the way that we read Daniel 3.
point_summary: Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego's refusal to bow—rather than accepting an easy deliverance from Nebuchadnezzar—is what made Daniel 3 the testimony it is, and preserved their confidence before God at His appearing.

context: The Bible says this matter is by the decree of the watchers and the demand by the word of the holy ones to the intent that the living may know.
point_summary: God's sovereign appointment of rulers — even the basest of men — proves that the Most High actively governs the kingdoms of men, and the living are meant to know this truth.

context: We read it in Daniel 3, Daniel 6.
point_summary: Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego's refusal to bow in Daniel 3 illustrates that true obedience to God is a matter of prior commitment, not convenience, holding firm even under threat of death.

context: We read it in Daniel 3, Daniel 6.
point_summary: Daniel's example of praying despite the lion's den threat illustrates that obedience to God is a matter of prior commitment, not convenience, holding firm when compliance becomes costly.

context: But there are four and they're walking around and the fourth is like unto the Son of God.
point_summary: Nebuchadnezzar's astonishment at a fourth figure "like unto the Son of God" in the furnace illustrates that God reveals Himself even to the proudest rulers, producing undeniable acknowledgment of who He is.

context: At Nebuchadnezzar in Daniel chapter 4, and we'll look at Daniel again in just a few moments.
point_summary: Nebuchadnezzar's boast over Babylon in Daniel 4:30 illustrates how unchecked pride—the same flesh-driven desire seen in Uzziah—leads a man to claim glory that belongs to God alone.

context: notice in Daniel 4, in verse number 30, notice what King Nebuchadnezzar says. It says that the king spake and said, is not this great Babylon that, notice when he says, I have built for the house of the kingdom by the might of my power and for the honor of my majesty.
point_summary: Nebuchadnezzar's boast — "I have built… by the might of my power" — illustrates the flesh's universal desire to claim personal glory for what God has accomplished, the very pride that must be mortified to walk humbly.

context: we read there of his elevating himself
point_summary: Nebuchadnezzar's self-glorying declaration in Daniel 4:30 illustrates the flesh's desire to claim personal credit, serving as a biblical example that pride precedes destruction.

context: While the word was in the king's mouth, there fell a voice from heaven.
point_summary: Nebuchadnezzar's judgment fell while his boast was still on his lips, illustrating that God moves swiftly to bring down pride and reclaim sovereign rule over human kingdoms.

context: O king Nebuchadnezzar, to thee it is spoken, the kingdom is departed from thee...
point_summary: Nebuchadnezzar's immediate judgment—driven from men to eat grass as oxen the same hour his boast left his mouth—illustrates that pride goes before destruction and that God alone rules kingdoms.

context: The same hour was the thing fulfilled upon Nebuchadnezzar...
point_summary: Nebuchadnezzar's immediate, same-hour judgment illustrates that pride before destruction is not a gradual process—God's discipline can fall while the boast is still on one's lips.

context: And the Bible says there in verse number 34, at the end of the day, as I, Nebuchadnezzar lifted...
point_summary: Nebuchadnezzar's humiliation and eventual restoration illustrate that pride brings swift divine judgment, and only after that destruction does he acknowledge God's sovereign rule.

context: A decision as the three Hebrew children did in Daniel 3, not to bow.
point_summary: Decisions that endure against temptation—like the three Hebrew children's refusal to bow—are grounded in the unchanging truth of God's Word.

context: And the Bible says that Nebuchadnezzar was astonished. He was a stone-eyed, the Bible says in Daniel chapter three and verse number 24.
point_summary: Nebuchadnezzar's astonishment at seeing the three men loosed and walking with a fourth like the Son of God illustrates how a surrendered life impacts even the lost.

context: those three Hebrew young men that he threw in the fiery furnace, that they were now loosed and they were walking around and there was a fourth that was like unto the Son of God.
point_summary: Absolute surrender to God—illustrated by Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego yielding their bodies in the fiery furnace—is confirmed by the presence of a fourth figure "like unto the Son of God."

context: Then Nebuchadnezzar spake and said, blessed be the God of Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego.
point_summary: Nebuchadnezzar's own words in verse 28 identify the defining characteristic of Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego as trust in God expressed through bodily yielding—the Old Testament picture of Romans 12:1's living sacrifice.

context: They said to Nebuchadnezzar before they were thrown into the fiery furnace that our God is able to deliver.
point_summary: Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego's willingness to be thrown into the furnace illustrates the meaning of "yielded"—surrendering one's body entirely to God regardless of outcome.

context: If you read Daniel 3, it's not what it says, amen?
point_summary: Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego's refusal to bow before Nebuchadnezzar's image illustrates that decisions which endure under pressure are grounded in obedience to the specific commands of God's Word.

context: Shadrach, Meshach and Abednego in that fiery furnace. We're mindful of Daniel three and we read and we rejoice in what took place and we find that Nebuchadnezzar says, hey, how many did we put in that fire? Three that were bound.
point_summary: The presence of Christ with his people across redemptive history is illustrated by Nebuchadnezzar's own testimony that a fourth figure, like the Son of God, walked with Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego in the furnace.

context: According to Daniel chapter number nine in verse number 27, he, the Antichrist, is going to sign a peace treaty with Israel at the start of the tribulation.
point_summary: Daniel 9:27 is read as predicting that the Antichrist will initiate a peace treaty with Israel at the tribulation's start, then break it at the three-and-a-half-year midpoint.

context: this is a term that is used by Daniel in Daniel 7 in verse number 13
point_summary: Daniel 7:13 is identified as the origin of the title "Son of Man," which Jesus applies to Himself throughout the Gospels, with the term carrying an emphasis on His humanity.

context: Daniel used it in Daniel 7, the emphasis upon the humanity of Jesus Christ.
point_summary: Daniel 7's title "Son of Man" is cited as emphasizing Jesus' humanity—the human form he took specifically to redeem mankind—while Mark 10 then demonstrates that this same Son of Man holds divine authority to forgive sins.

context: Turn back with me if you will to the book of Daniel...this morning I want you to look at Daniel chapter number nine...
point_summary: Daniel 9:24 is cited to show that reconciliation and the end of sins were prophetically determined by God, connecting the Old Testament promise to the finished atoning work of Christ at Calvary.

context: The Bible says this 70 weeks are determined upon thy people and upon thy holy city to finish the transgression And to make an end of sins notice this and to make reconciliation for iniquity and to bring in Everlasting righteousness and to seal up the vision and prophecy and to anoint the most holy
point_summary: Daniel 9:24's prophecy that Messiah would be "cut off but not for himself" grounds the sermon's claim that reconciliation for iniquity was accomplished through Christ's substitutionary death on the cross.

context: And after three score and two weeks shall Messiah be cut off but not for himself and the people of the Prince that shall come shall destroy the city and the Sanctuary and the end thereof shall be with a flood and unto the end of the war desolations are determined
point_summary: Daniel 9:26's prophecy that Messiah would be "cut off but not for himself" is presented as Old Testament confirmation of substitutionary atonement, anticipating Paul's declaration that Christ "died for all."

context: Daniel understood in Daniel 9 or 10, he understood by reading of the books that 70 years were accomplished
point_summary: Daniel's recognition of the seventy-year captivity in Daniel 9 is grounded directly in Leviticus 26, where God prescribed that the land would rest during Israel's exile to compensate for neglected sabbath years.

context: You read Daniel's prayer in Daniel chapter number nine, it's exactly related to what we're reading in Leviticus 26.
point_summary: Daniel 9's prayer of national confession is presented as a direct parallel to Leviticus 26, illustrating that covenantal repentance and God's remembrance of His covenant are inseparably linked.

context: We see that in Daniel 9. We see that in Nehemiah 1, where they're confessing, they're saying, we, God, have forsaken you
point_summary: Daniel 9 is cited alongside Nehemiah 1 as a biblical example of the confessional repentance—acknowledging sin and turning back to God—that Leviticus 26 prescribes as the solution to national judgment.

context: I'm thankful that there are some young Hebrew boys that were willing to go into a fiery furnace that we read about in Daniel chapter number 3
point_summary: Daniel 3 is cited as proof that God does not merely declare His power in theory but demonstrates it through the trials of His people, with Nebuchadnezzar's own confession as the result.

context: what about Darius in Daniel chapter number 6? He says, I know your God's able to deliver thee
point_summary: Trials like Daniel's den of lions are cited as proof that Scripture is a practical book, not mere theory — God demonstrating his power through adversity so that even Darius could declare him able to deliver.

context: we understand Daniel's prophecy in Daniel chapter number nine
point_summary: Daniel 9's prophecy is cited as the basis for understanding that the Rapture and the Second Coming are two distinct events separated by seven years.

context: We think about Daniel in Daniel 6. Daniel had a hardship.
point_summary: Hardships can be the direct consequence of faithful obedience, as Daniel's very walk with God was what led him into the lions' den.

context: ...I'm thankful that Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego didn't live that way. You know why? We wouldn't have Daniel to read about that situation...
point_summary: Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego's refusal to manipulate their circumstances illustrates that self-reliant intervention limits what God is able to do, whereas faith leaves room for God to act.

context: ...I'm thankful that Daniel didn't act that way. We wouldn't have, that's Daniel 3, then you look at Daniel 6 and the den of lions...
point_summary: Daniel's refusal to compromise in the lions' den is cited as evidence that trusting God rather than manipulating circumstances allows God to act in ways that would otherwise be forfeited.

context: for the passage of Scripture that we're going to look at this morning in Daniel 3, we find that these three Hebrew children that we often are familiar with, Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego, these fellas, they faced unique circumstances
point_summary: Daniel 3 is introduced as a cross-generational model of faithfulness under pressure, with Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego exemplifying the willingness to stand against the ideological conformity demanded by their culture.

context: We find that as we look at a passage like Daniel 3, we rejoice greatly in understanding what takes place, and we're familiar with the passage of Scripture, and we say, man, this is wonderful, look at how God worked, look at what God did, but it's because there were some people of God that stood, that were willing to take a stand no matter what the pressures were, no matter what the consequences were that they would face, they were willing to take a stand
point_summary: Daniel 3 is presented as a cross-generational pattern in which God's people face cultural pressure to conform, and the faithfulness of Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego to stand regardless of consequences becomes the model and question for every subsequent generation.

context: Nebuchadnezzar the king made an image of gold whose height was three square cubits and the breadth thereof six cubits. He set it up in the plain of Dura in the province of Babylon.
point_summary: Nebuchadnezzar's erection of a ninety-foot golden image establishes the coercive power of the state that God's people in every generation must decide whether to resist.

context: the circumstances that are faced in Daniel 3 are different
point_summary: Daniel 3 is presented as a historical example of standing for truth under powerful opposition, with the expectation that each generation faces its own circumstances requiring the same willingness to stand.

context: And here in Daniel 3, we find as we've read in verse number one that this golden image is set up
point_summary: Nebuchadnezzar's erection of a ninety-foot golden image in Babylon's plain of Dura establishes the coercive, empire-backed circumstances of idol worship that each generation faces in its own form.

context: in verse 2 it says then Nebuchadnezzar the king sent to gather together, notice, the princes, the governors, and the captains
point_summary: Nebuchadnezzar's summons of every rank of provincial official to the image's dedication illustrates the totalizing, empire-wide pressure to conform that these three Hebrew men were called to resist.

context: Thou, O king, hast made a decree that every man that shall hear the sound of the cornet, flute, harp, sakhba, psaltery, and dulcimer, and all kinds of music, shall fall down and worship the golden image.
point_summary: Nebuchadnezzar's decree, restated by the Chaldean accusers in verse 10, frames the deliberate defiance of Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego as a conscious, costly stand against a known and enforced command.

context: And whoso falleth not down and worshipeth that he should be cast in the midst of a burning fiery furnace.
point_summary: Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego's refusal to bow—despite the known decree and its deadly consequence—is presented as a deliberate, courageous stand against Nebuchadnezzar's command.

context: There are certain Jews, in verse number 12, whom thou hast set over the affairs of the province of Babylon. Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego. These men, O king, have not regarded thee. They serve not thy gods, nor worship the golden image which thou hast set up.
point_summary: Daniel 3:12 marks the moment Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego are publicly accused of refusing to bow—identified as the stand they took against Nebuchadnezzar's decree.

context: Is it true, O Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego, do not ye serve my gods, nor worship the golden image which I have set up?
point_summary: Nebuchadnezzar's interrogation of the three men—dragged before the most powerful ruler on earth in his fury—frames the moment as a test of whether they will bow under the ultimate pressure of threatened death.

context: Now, if ye be ready, that at what time ye hear the sound of the cornet, flute, harp, sackbutt, psalter, and dulcimer, and all kinds of music, ye fall down and worship the image which I have made well. But if you worship not, ye should be cast the same hour into the midst of a burning fiery furnace. Notice the question that ends verse number 15. And who is that God that shall deliver you out of my hands?
point_summary: Nebuchadnezzar's closing taunt — "who is that God that shall deliver you out of my hands?" — exposes the king's pride as he offers a second chance to bow, framing compromise as the path to comfort and ease.

context: if he doesn't deliver us out of the burning fiery furnace, they knew that he would deliver him out of the hand of the king
point_summary: Confidence in God's ability to deliver—not certainty about *how* He would act—grounded the three men's refusal to bow, rooted in faith that God was able even if the outcome remained unknown.

context: But if not, be it known unto thee, O king
point_summary: Even if God chose not to deliver them from the furnace, the three men were fully resolved not to bow — their confidence rested in both what God could do and what God had commanded.

context: we will not serve thy gods, nor worship the golden image which thou hast set up
point_summary: Even if God chose not to deliver them from the furnace, the three men were fully resolved not to bow — their confidence rested in both what God could do and what God had commanded.

context: Nebuchadnezzar was full of fury in verse number 19. The form of his visage was changed.
point_summary: Nebuchadnezzar's fury at the three men's refusal to bow escalated to the point of heating the furnace seven times hotter and deploying his mightiest soldiers—raising the stakes of their stand to the extreme.

context: I believe in verse number 13, when it says that he was in his rage and fury that he brought him before him
point_summary: Nebuchadnezzar's rage in verse 13, though fierce, still included a second chance to bow, making his later fury in verse 19 the point of no return when the three men stood firm again.

context: then these men were bound in their coats, their hosen, their hats, their other garments, and were cast in the midst of the burning fiery furnace
point_summary: The furnace was so superheated by Nebuchadnezzar's rage that the soldiers who cast the three bound men in were themselves killed by the flames, underscoring the extremity of the threat the three faced.

context: The Bible tells us in verse number 24 that he rose up in haste, and he speaks to his counselors
point_summary: Nebuchadnezzar's astonished question — confirming three bound men were cast in — sets up the miracle's credibility through the king's own witness and his counselors' corroboration.

context: did not we cast three men bound into the midst of the fire? They answered and said unto the king, true, O king.
point_summary: Nebuchadnezzar's astonished question—weren't three men bound and cast in?—sets up the miracle: the men are now loose, unhurt, and walking with a fourth figure like the Son of God.

context: lo, I see four men loose, walking in the midst of the fire, and they have no hurt; and the form of the fourth is like the Son of God.
point_summary: The miraculous presence of a fourth figure "like the Son of God" in the furnace illustrates that faithful obedience to God, even unto death, can produce a communion with Him beyond ordinary comprehension.

context: And I believe that as I look at a passage like Daniel 3, that I'm encouraged.
point_summary: Standing firm for God may bring fiery consequences, but Daniel 3 promises divine presence in the trial and seasons of communion sweeter than any known before.

context: But in verse 27, I want you to notice how this experience impacted the lives of the princes, the governors, the captains, and the king's counselors. Notice what it says in verse 27. Again, to get back to the context, Nebuchadnezzar has come close to the mouth of the burning fiery furnace. He says to these men, ye servants of the Most High God, come forth, and they came hither. They came forth out of the midst of the fire. It says in verse 27, and the princes, governors, and captains, and the king's counselors, being gathered together, notice these three words, saw these men. They saw it. They were eyewitnesses of these men walking out of the burning fiery furnace. And it says, upon whose bodies the fire had no power,
point_summary: The miraculous preservation of Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego—not a hair singed, no smell of smoke—was witnessed firsthand by Babylon's highest officials, making their deliverance an undeniable public testimony.

context: It says in verse 29, therefore, I make a decree. Now, he started the chapter by making a decree that everybody bow down to his image, and now he makes a decree at the end of the chapter that every people, nation, and language would speak anything amiss against the God of Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego shall be cut in pieces, and their houses shall be made a dunghill. Why? Because there is no other God that can deliver after this sort.
point_summary: Nebuchadnezzar's forced decree protecting the God of Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego demonstrates that faithful standing results in God being glorified, not merely the courage of those who stood.

context: I'm encouraged by Daniel 3, but really more than being encouraged, I'm challenged.
point_summary: Daniel 3 challenges believers not merely to admire Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego's resolve but to match it—standing publicly for a God the watching world considers unreal, amid contemporary pressures to conform and be silenced.

context: So the Bible tells us in Daniel 6 and verse number 10, now when Daniel knew that the writing was signed...
point_summary: Daniel's deliberate, fearless return to his established place of prayer—even after knowing the king's decree was signed—illustrates that a structured, habitual prayer life perseveres regardless of personal cost.

context: I'm reminded of those three Hebrew children in the book of Daniel, when they stand before Nebuchadnezzar, the powerful king of Babylon, and they say, our God is able.
point_summary: Daniel 3:17 is invoked as the biblical anchor for the claim that God is able to deliver his people, whether through the storm or from it, even if deliverance ultimately means death and entrance into his presence.

context: In Daniel 9 it prophesies of the timing in which Jesus would be born.
point_summary: Daniel 9 is cited as the prophetic text that foretold not merely that Messiah would come, but the specific timing of His birth.

context: ...our God answers prayer. We preached that message last Sunday morning from Daniel 2...
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: he says bow to this image, the three Hebrew children that refused to bow, and he says, who is that God that will deliver you out of my hand?
point_summary: Nebuchadnezzar's defiant question—"Who is that God that shall deliver you out of my hands?"—mirrors Pharaoh's ignorance of God, both rulers soon compelled to discover His sovereign power.

context: didn't we throw three in there, bound? But there's four walking around and the fourth is like unto the Son of God, the Son of Man.
point_summary: God's self-revelation to those who defy Him is illustrated by Nebuchadnezzar's encounter with the fourth figure in the furnace, paralleling Pharaoh's forced recognition of the Lord through the plagues.

context: Nebuchadnezzar over in the book of Daniel
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Daniel chapter number 9 is the end of the chapter, deals with the 70-week prophecy of Daniel. We've mentioned that in the past. We're familiar with some of the detail of that. And I want you to look in this passage of Scripture, we're just going to look at verse number 27 for sake of time here, but I want you to look at what God tells us is going to happen.
point_summary: Daniel 9:27 is read as the Antichrist's covenant with many — a seven-year peace treaty whose inauguration explains how Israel comes to dwell in the perceived safety described in Ezekiel 38.

context: It says this in verse number 27, speaking of the antichrist, it says, and he shall confirm the covenant with many for one week. In the midst of the week he shall cause the sacrifice and the oblation to cease, and for the overspreading of abominations he shall make it desolate, even until the consummation and that determined shall be poured upon the desolate.
point_summary: Daniel 9:27 is read as the prophetic basis for a peace covenant between Israel and the Antichrist that inaugurates the seven-year tribulation, with its breaking at the midpoint signaling the onset of desolation.

context: if you look in Daniel 5 in verse number 1 The Bible says that Belshazzar the king made a great feast to a thousand of his Lords and drank wine before the thousand
point_summary: Belshazzar's feast in Daniel 5 is cited as a historical precedent for Babylon's sudden, unexpected judgment, paralleling the swift fall of commercial and religious Babylon in Revelation 18.

context: It says in verse number five in the same hour Came forth fingers of a man's hand and wrote over against the candlestick upon the plaster the wall of the king's palace and the king Saw the part of the hand that wrote
point_summary: Daniel 5:5's account of sudden divine judgment on Babylon mid-feast is invoked as a historical precedent for the swift, unexpected fall of Babylon described in Revelation 17–18.

context: Darius says, listen, I know that your God, whom you serve continually, is able to deliver you.
point_summary: Even a pagan king's confidence that Daniel's God "is able to deliver" illustrates the broader truth that God's power to rescue His people is undeniable and far-reaching.

context: he comes to that place and he says, hey Daniel, is your God, has He delivered you?
point_summary: Daniel's deliverance from the lions' den, confirmed by Darius's own words, illustrates that God is able to deliver His people, reinforcing the conditional promise of deliverance Samuel declared to Israel.

context: I want you to turn over to Daniel chapter three, if you will. It's not really about Daniel. It's about his three friends in Daniel chapter number three.
point_summary: Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego's refusal to bow to Nebuchadnezzar's image illustrates what it means to yield to God rather than to an earthly authority demanding worship.

context: Daniel, of course, we find the great truth in Daniel six
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: But in Daniel chapter three, we see that Nebuchadnezzar set up this great statue...
point_summary: Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego's refusal to bow—trusting God to deliver them yet yielding even if He did not—illustrates what it means to yield oneself wholly to God as Lord.

context: If it be so, our God whom we serve is able to deliver us from the burning, fiery furnace, and he will deliver us out of thine hand, O king.
point_summary: Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego's refusal to bow illustrates that God is able to deliver his people from any trial, yet sometimes he chooses to deliver them *through* it rather than from it.

context: But if not, be it known unto thee, O king, that we will not serve thy gods, nor worship the golden image which thou hast set up.
point_summary: Even when God is able to deliver his people *from* a trial, he sometimes chooses instead to deliver them *through* it, as Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego's unwavering refusal to bow demonstrates.

context: We see it in Daniel 3.
point_summary: Yielding oneself fully to God, even unto death, brings glory to God—as seen when Nebuchadnezzar declared there is no God like this God after the three men's faithfulness in Daniel 3.

context: And you read in Daniel chapter six and you read all that's taking place with the writing and the fact that they could not make any petitions of any kings or gods and so on and so forth.
point_summary: Daniel 6:7 is cited briefly as background to the point that Daniel continued praying despite the decree, illustrating that God is able to deliver those who trust Him rather than earthly protection.

context: And you find that Daniel's going and he's praying and he's seeking the Lord.
point_summary: Daniel 6:10 is cited briefly to illustrate that Daniel prayed and sought the Lord amid opposition, reinforcing the broader point that God is able to answer those who call on Him in faith.

context: And of course they come and they accuse him and he's going to go into the den of lions.
point_summary: Daniel 6:16's record of Darius confessing "thy God whom thou servest continually, he will deliver thee" is cited as the biblical grounding for the conviction that God is able—the same faith Ezra's company had to live out by seeking divine protection rather than a royal military escort.

context: And the Bible tells us that the king Darius, he says, I know that your God is able.
point_summary: Daniel's declaration that God "is able" — echoed in Ephesians 3:20 and Darius's words in Daniel 6 — called Ezra's company to live by faith rather than seek military escort, trusting God's proven sufficiency.

context: Daniel chapter number six the events in Daniel six are quite familiar. We won't be looking at them this morning, but Daniel chapter number six, remember there was a law that was signed into writing that no one was allowed to ask any petitions of anyone other than the King. And when Daniel knew that the writing was signed, what did he do? Well, he went in and he prayed.
point_summary: Daniel's defiant, uninterrupted prayer three times daily—even after the king's decree—demonstrates that a genuine prayer life is marked by consistency and fearlessness before God.

context: He kneeled down on his knees and the Bible tells us that three times a day he prayed and gave thanks before his God. But there's a little phrase that at the end of verse number 10 of Daniel six that says this, as he did a four time.
point_summary: Daniel's defiant prayer after the king's decree was not a reaction to crisis but a continuation of lifelong habit, as the phrase "as he did aforetime" reveals his prayer life predated and outlasted any law against it.

context: We know in Daniel chapter number nine, as you read the prayer of Daniel,
point_summary: Daniel 9 is cited as evidence that Daniel was a man of prayer, with his recorded prayer there described as powerful and marked by fasting, sackcloth, and ashes.

context: Daniel nine records a powerful prayer in Daniel's life.
point_summary: Daniel 9 is cited as evidence that Daniel was a man of consistent, fervent prayer, with his recorded prayer of fasting, sackcloth, and ashes demonstrating that he genuinely believed God hears and answers prayer.

context: examine an event in Daniel's life that's recorded for us in Daniel chapter two.
point_summary: Daniel 2 is introduced specifically to examine the prayer Daniel offered in response to Nebuchadnezzar's impossible demand, establishing Daniel as a man who believed God hears and answers prayer.

context: Begin looking there with me at verse number seven.
point_summary: Daniel 2:7 introduces the wise men's repeated demand that Nebuchadnezzar first tell them the dream, setting up the king's accusation that they are stalling for time.

context: The Chaldeans answered in verse number 10 before the king and said, there is not a man upon the earth that can show the king's matter.
point_summary: The Chaldeans' confession that no earthly man could reveal the king's matter sets up the contrast with Daniel, to whom God alone could disclose what was hidden.

context: then was the secret revealed unto Daniel in a night vision
point_summary: God's secret, which no earthly wise man could reveal, was disclosed directly to Daniel through a night vision, demonstrating that divine knowledge surpasses all human wisdom.

context: Then Daniel blessed the God of heaven
point_summary: God's direct revelation to Daniel in a night vision — not human wisdom — is what made the impossible possible, contrasting sharply with the Chaldeans' confession that no man on earth could reveal such a secret.

context: Nebuchadnezzar had a dream according to verse number one
point_summary: Nebuchadnezzar's dream in Daniel 2:1 sets the crisis in motion: his demand that the wise men reveal both the dream and its interpretation—not merely interpret a dream he told them—exposed the limits of human wisdom.

context: The Bible tells us that the king responds, notice in verse number 12, for this cause the king was angry and very furious and commanded to destroy all the wise men of Babylon.
point_summary: Nebuchadnezzar's rage at the wise men's failure produces an irrational death decree that sweeps in Daniel himself, though he was unaware of the crisis that triggered it.

context: why is the decree so hasty from the king? Then Ariok made the thing known to Daniel.
point_summary: Daniel's request for time from the king, rather than immediate panic or flight, is presented as the faith-driven first step that made his subsequent prayer in verses 17–18 possible.

context: number two this morning in verses 17 and 18, I want you to notice not only do we see, first of all, the situation reported, but we see number two, the supplication recorded.
point_summary: Daniel's response to crisis—going immediately to his companions for united prayer—illustrates that the proper first recourse when facing impossible circumstances is corporate supplication to God.

context: number two this morning in verses 17 and 18, I want you to notice not only do we see, first of all, the situation reported, but we see number two, the supplication recorded.
point_summary: Faced with a crisis demanding divine knowledge, Daniel's immediate response was corporate prayer — gathering his companions to seek God's mercy for revelation they could not produce on their own.

context: In Daniel chapter number two, if Daniel would have responded with worry, with full of care, how would that have helped him?
point_summary: Daniel's crisis in chapter 2 illustrates Philippians 4:6 in practice: rather than responding to mortal danger with worry, Daniel called a prayer meeting, modeling the command to bring every need to God.

context: I've never received news like Daniel received in Daniel chapter number two.
point_summary: When Daniel faced a death sentence, his response was to call a prayer meeting, illustrating that prayer—not worry—is the proper recourse because God can actually act on the requests made to Him.

context: when those three same companions stand before Nebuchadnezzar because they didn't bow before the image
point_summary: The companions' declaration in Daniel 3:18 that God is able to deliver—and their resolve to stand firm even if He does not—illustrates the believing, unconditional faith that the sermon holds up as the model for Daniel's own prayer in chapter 2.

context: the Bible says in verse number 2 of our passage of scripture in Daniel 2 that he, Nebuchadnezzar, he got all the astrologers, the sorcerers, the magicians and all the Chaldeans
point_summary: Daniel 2:2 is cited to contrast the total inability of Nebuchadnezzar's wisest human counselors with what only God could do—establishing the impossibility that makes Daniel's faith and God's revelation all the more significant.

context: notice what he says in verse 28. There is a God in heaven. There is a God in heaven that revealeth secrets.
point_summary: Daniel's declaration before Nebuchadnezzar — "there is a God in heaven that revealeth secrets" — is cited as evidence that Daniel prayed with faith, knowing the answer could only come from God, not from human ability.

context: Then was the secret revealed unto Daniel. I mean, hallelujah that we serve a God who hears and answers prayer, that he worked in Daniel's life and that he still works today in your life and in my life and he answers prayer. But I want you to notice something extremely important with regard to the subject matter of prayer as we look in Daniel chapter number two.
point_summary: God's direct answer to Daniel's corporate prayer — revealing the dream and its interpretation in a night vision — demonstrates that he hears and responds to those who seek his mercy concerning what only he knows.

context: Daniel answered and said, blessed be the name of God forever and ever for wisdom and might are His.
point_summary: Daniel's blessing of God's name "forever and ever" in verse 20 grounds his worship in God's eternal nature, omniscience, and omnipotence—attributes that moved Daniel to stop and rejoice before acting, even amid urgent, life-threatening circumstances.

context: And so Daniel, he's rejoicing. He says this, he giveth wisdom unto the wise, and knowledge to them that know understanding.
point_summary: God's sovereign control over times, seasons, and rulers—illustrated by Nebuchadnezzar's humbling—grounds Daniel's praise that God grants wisdom and knowledge to those who seek Him.

context: I thank thee and praise thee, O thou God of my fathers, notice who has given me wisdom and might.
point_summary: Daniel's thanksgiving in verse 23 models the discipline of pausing to credit God—not human wisdom—when prayer is answered, a pattern the sermon presents as essential but easily neglected.

context: therefore Daniel went in unto Ariok, whom the king had ordained to destroy the wise men of Babylon.
point_summary: Daniel's confidence before Arioch to spare the wise men and present the interpretation flows directly from his prior acknowledgment that God, not human wisdom, had revealed the secret.

context: Daniel says that there is a God in heaven that revealeth secrets.
point_summary: God's revelation of secrets to Daniel—cited from Daniel 2:28—grounds the sermon's call to prayer in the conviction that we serve the same God who reveals hidden things and is able to deliver.

context: Darius had did everything within his power to deliver Daniel from having to go into the den of lions, we find that he says that thou, your God who you serve continually, He'll deliver thee.
point_summary: Daniel 6:16 is invoked as evidence that God is able to deliver His servants, with Darius's words to Daniel confirming that the God served continually will deliver — illustrating the same delivering power Daniel trusted in chapter 2.

point_summary: Daniel's response to understanding Jeremiah's 70-year prophecy was immediate prayer, fasting, and seeking God for answers about what was taking place.

context: 70 weeks are determined upon thy people and upon thy holy city to finish the transgression and to make an end of sins and to make reconciliation for iniquity and to bring in everlasting righteousness and to seal up the vision and prophecy and to anoint the most holy
point_summary: Daniel 9:24's six-fold purpose clause—finishing transgression, ending sins, making reconciliation, bringing everlasting righteousness, sealing vision and prophecy, and anointing the most holy—is presented as the divine "why" behind the seventy weeks before the sermon turns to address the "when."

context: from the going forth of the commandment to restore and to build Jerusalem unto the Messiah, the Prince

context: shall be seven weeks and threescore and two weeks. The street shall be built again and the wall even in troubleless times. And after threescore and two weeks shall Messiah be cut off, but not for himself

context: And the people of the Prince that shall come shall destroy the city and the sanctuary and the end thereof shall be with a flood and unto the end of the war, desolations are determined
point_summary: Daniel 9:26 identifies the Messiah being "cut off" and the subsequent destruction of the city and sanctuary as specific events filling out the detailed timeline that verse 24's general overview only introduces.

context: and he shall confirm the covenant with many for one week. point_summary: Verse 27's "he" is identified with the Prince of verse 26, a figure who confirms a covenant, then breaks it mid-week by halting sacrifice and bringing desolating abominations.

context: verse number 24 of Daniel nine provides a general overview of this entire prophecy
point_summary: Daniel 9:24 functions as a panoramic summary of the entire 70-weeks prophecy, with verses 25–27 supplying the sequential details and specific intervals within that overarching framework.

context: the events that are recorded here in Daniel 9 were concluded or fulfilled in 490 days
point_summary: The "seventy weeks" of Daniel 9:24 refers to 490 years, not 490 days, a conclusion confirmed by fulfilled history and by the impossibility of the recorded events occurring within a 490-day span.

context: the book of Daniel gives us kind of a key to understanding what we're going to study here in the weeks ahead in the book of Revelation
point_summary: The "seventy weeks" of Daniel 9:24 are interpreted as 490 years—not days—confirmed both by Daniel's own year-oriented thinking in verse 2 and by the historical impossibility of fulfilling the recorded events within 490 days.

context: in verse 24, the Bible says 70 weeks are determined upon thy people
point_summary: The phrase "thy people" and "thy holy city" in Daniel 9:24 identifies the 490-year prophecy as specifically concerning Israel and Jerusalem, not the church or any other nation.

context: and upon thy holy city
point_summary: The "seventy weeks" of Daniel 9:24 are identified as 490 literal years specifically concerning the Jewish people and Jerusalem, establishing the prophecy's ethnic and geographic scope before its timeline is examined.

context: verse 25 specifically gives us the key that starts the clock. Notice in verse 25 again, he starts out by saying, know therefore and understand. And then notice the very next statement that from the going forth of the commandment to restore and to build Jerusalem
point_summary: Daniel 9:25 identifies the specific commandment to restore and build Jerusalem—not the temple—as the event that starts the 490-year prophetic clock.

context: as we look at Daniel chapter nine and look at all that's taking place

point_summary: Artaxerxes' decree to Nehemiah to restore and build Jerusalem marks the precise starting point—"the going forth of the commandment"—from which Daniel's prophetic clock begins counting.

context: And you begin to understand that not only the prophecies that we're reading here of Daniel 9

context: that revelation in Daniel 9, 24, 27, would be completed
point_summary: The unfulfilled scope of Daniel 9:24–27 requires a gap between the 69th and 70th weeks, since a continuous timeline would have completed the prophecy by 37 AD, which it was not.

context: that revelation in Daniel 9, 24, 27, would be completed
point_summary: The unfulfilled 70th week of Daniel 9:24–27 requires a gap between the 69th and 70th weeks—a gap identified as the church age, beginning at Pentecost and ending at the rapture.

context: The Bible says, as we pick it back up in Daniel 9 and verse 26, and after threescore and two weeks shall Messiah be cut off, but not for himself
point_summary: Since the 69 weeks were fulfilled literally, the 70th week demands the same literal interpretation, including the coming prince of Daniel 9:26 being identified as Antichrist, not Christ.

context: and the people of the prince that shall come, notice it says shall destroy the city and the sanctuary
point_summary: Consistent literal fulfillment of the 69 weeks demands that the "prince that shall come" in verse 26 refers to the Antichrist, not the Lord, whose people destroy the city and sanctuary.

context: and the people of the prince that shall come shall destroy the city and the sanctuary, and the end thereof shall be with a flood, and unto the end of the war desolations are determined
point_summary: Consistent literal fulfillment of the 69 weeks demands that the "prince that shall come" in verse 26—identified here as Antichrist, not Christ—and the 70th week be interpreted with equal literalness.

context: it says in Daniel 9 27 he speaking of the Antichrist shall confirm the covenant with many for one

context: we're mindful this evening of Daniel, in Daniel chapter number six
point_summary: Daniel's continued prayer in defiance of the royal decree, and his subsequent deliverance from the lions' den, is presented as a living example of God's ability to deliver those who call upon Him in trouble.

context: we find in Daniel chapter number six, there was a day in which Daniel was made aware of this signing, this decree
point_summary: Daniel's continued prayer despite the king's decree, and his subsequent deliverance from the lions' den, is presented as a living illustration of God's power to deliver His people in days of trouble.

context: in verse 16 of Daniel six, the Bible says, then the King commanded and they brought Daniel and cast him into the den of lions
point_summary: Even a pagan king, unable to deliver Daniel despite his best efforts, confessed that the God Daniel served continually would accomplish what human power could not.

context: if you look at the end of verse number 14, when the King heard these words, he was sore displeased with himself
point_summary: Even a king who labored until sundown and was displeased with himself could not deliver Daniel, underscoring that true deliverance belonged only to the God Daniel served continually.

context: we pick up the text in verse number 20, when he came to the den, he cried with a lamentable voice unto Daniel
point_summary: Daniel's deliverance from the lions' den — with the King himself testifying that the God Daniel served continually was able to deliver him — illustrates that God answers His people in their day of trouble.

context: O Daniel, servant of the living God, is thy God whom thou service continually able to deliver thee from the lions? Then said Daniel unto the King, O King, live forever. My God hath sent his angel and hath shut the lion's mouth that they have not hurt me
point_summary: Daniel's answer to the king from inside the lion's den demonstrates that God delivered him in his trouble, fulfilling the very promise the king himself had spoken over him.

context: Because Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego stood up tall when they played the music. Amen? They didn't bow down
point_summary: Visible, countercultural faithfulness—illustrated by Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego refusing to bow—is the expected evidence that genuine faith makes believers noticeably different from the world around them.

context: I think of Daniel in Daniel chapter number six. That poor king, he tried, he did everything he could to keep Daniel from going to the den of lions
point_summary: Daniel's deliverance from the lions' den illustrates that God is able to do what no earthly king—not even Darius—could do: supernaturally restrain what threatens His servant's life.

context: Daniel answered and said, blessed be the name of God forever and ever, for wisdom and might are his. And he changeth the times and the seasons. He removeth kings and setteth up kings. He giveth wisdom unto the wise and knowledge to them that know understanding.
point_summary: God's sovereignty over times, seasons, and the rise and fall of kings means that no earthly chaos catches Him off guard or exceeds His absolute control.

context: That's why he tried to silence Daniel.
point_summary: Daniel's example of persistent prayer, despite opposition, is cited as evidence that the devil knows prayer works and therefore seeks to silence it.

reference_text: Daniel 6 (Daniel in the den of lions)
context: Daniel is spared from the den of lions
point_summary: God's goodness holds equally in miraculous deliverance—Daniel from the lions' den—and in martyrdom, since both outcomes occur within a sin-cursed world that has rejected God.

reference_text: Daniel 3 (Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego in the fiery furnace)
context: Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego are delivered from a fiery furnace
point_summary: God's goodness holds equally in miraculous deliverance—Daniel's lions' den, the fiery furnace, the stilled sea—and in Stephen's martyrdom, because every outcome falls within the care of a God who does all things well.

context: when they looked into that furnace, they didn't just see the three men. They saw a fourth. And the Lord was in the fire with them
point_summary: God's presence with the three Hebrews in the furnace—seen as a fourth figure—illustrates that He sometimes withholds earlier deliverance so His people emerge with a testimony only the fire could produce.

context: He opened those shutters and looked east and began to pray to his God
point_summary: Daniel's deliberate, open prayer in defiance of the king's decree illustrates that faithful obedience sometimes leads deeper into trial before God ultimately delivers.

context: He goes all the way to the lion's den and is cast in the lion's den. And God shuts the mouths of the lion.
point_summary: Daniel's deliverance from the lions' den came only after he endured the full ordeal, illustrating that God sometimes withholds early intervention because He is more concerned with building character than ensuring comfort.

context: the same thing could be said of him, of the three Hebrew children. It was scary going in. He didn't know what God was going to do. He thought he might be ripped apart.
point_summary: God's delay in rescuing the three Hebrew children from the furnace was purposeful, producing a testimony of His faithfulness they could never have gained had He intervened before they entered the fire.

context: furnace praising the Lord
point_summary: The three Hebrew children emerging from the furnace illustrates that waiting on God through suffering yields praise, not regret — they came out glad He let them go through it.

context: drawn up out of the lion's den praising the Lord
point_summary: Being drawn from the lion's den praising God illustrates that waiting on the Lord through suffering, rather than manipulating outcomes, brings deliverance at His timing.

context: when Daniel is reading Jeremiah and it reveals to him the fact that their 70 years are accomplished and God gives him the vision
point_summary: Daniel's reading of Jeremiah's prophecy is cited to illustrate how the 70-year Babylonian captivity was the direct consequence of Israel's disobedience, confirming that God's judgment, not abandonment, drove their exile.

context: He is the leader of the world and in just a moment because of his pride, he's out in the middle of a field like a beast
point_summary: Nebuchadnezzar's sudden reduction from world ruler to beast-like wanderer serves as a warning against attributing one's abilities and provision to oneself rather than to God.

context: this matter is by the decree of the watchers and the demand by thy word of the holy ones to the intent that the living may know that the most high ruleth in the kingdom of men and giveth it to whomsoever he will
point_summary: God's sovereign appointment of rulers — including Joseph's elevation — is grounded in Daniel 4:17's declaration that the Most High rules in the kingdom of men and gives authority to whomever He wills.

context: the living may know that the most high ruleth in the kingdom of men and he giveth it to whomsoever he will and setteth up over it the basest of men
point_summary: Daniel 4:17 is cited to establish that God sovereignly rules over the kingdoms of men, appointing whomever He wills—including the basest of men—so that the living may know He alone is in control.

context: Every time we sing the song, I think about Daniel. And then I'm reminded that he's able to deliver me from what, from anything.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: that 70th week of Daniel, that that 70th week ends in Revelation 19
point_summary: The 70th week of Daniel is identified as the seven-year tribulation period that concludes in Revelation 19 with the battle of Armageddon and Christ's return with His saints.

context: if you were to read Daniel chapter number nine, and you read the prophecy of Daniel's 70 weeks
point_summary: Daniel's 70th week—the tribulation period—is identified as divided into two equal halves of three and a half years each, and its purpose is understood to concern Israel specifically, as confirmed by Jeremiah's phrase "the time of Jacob's trouble."

context: when we're speaking about the tribulation, we're speaking about Daniel's 70th week
point_summary: Daniel's 70th week — divided into two equal halves of three and a half years — is identified as the tribulation period, purposed entirely for Israel as "the time of Jacob's trouble."

context: If you look at Daniel's prophecy of those 70 weeks, there's a gap between week 69 and week 70.
point_summary: Daniel 9:24's seventy weeks prophecy is applied here to show that the present church age occupies a gap between the 69th and 70th weeks, during which Israel's partial spiritual blindness persists until the fullness of the Gentiles comes in.

context: if you were to look at Daniel's prophecy of the 70 weeks, it all has to do with Israel from the rebuilding of the walls
point_summary: Daniel's 70-weeks prophecy contains a gap between the 69th and 70th weeks—the present church age—during which Israel's partial spiritual blindness persists until the fullness of the Gentiles comes in.

context: in Daniel chapter nine, in verse 27, we're told that there's a covenant that is made with Israel
point_summary: Daniel 9:27's covenant marking the start of the 70th week is cited to argue that any non-pretribulation rapture position would allow believers to calculate Christ's return, undermining imminence.

context: I'll just look just a few verses earlier in verse 24
point_summary: Daniel 9:24's reference to seventy weeks "determined upon thy people and upon thy holy city" is used to establish that the prophecy concerns Israel and Jerusalem specifically, framing the 70th week as the basis for arguing that a non-pretribulation rapture would allow the date of Christ's return to be calculated, undermining imminence.

context: In Daniel nine, in verse 24, notice what the Bible says. 70 weeks are determined upon thy people and upon thy holy city
point_summary: Daniel 9:24–27 is read as a structured prophetic timeline with defined events at the start, midpoint, and end of the 70th week, making the precise calculation of Christ's return possible if the rapture occurs at any point other than before the tribulation.

context: Know therefore and understand that from the going forth of the commandment to restore and to build Jerusalem unto the Messiah the Prince shall be seven weeks and three score and two weeks
point_summary: Daniel 9:25's timeline of seven weeks plus sixty-two weeks from the commandment to restore Jerusalem until Messiah is cut off establishes the precise chronological structure that makes mid- or post-trib rapture positions mathematically falsifiable.

context: after three score and two weeks shall Messiah be cut off but not for himself
point_summary: Daniel 9:26's prophecy of Messiah being "cut off" and the city and sanctuary destroyed establishes fixed, calculable events within the 70-week timeline, making a non-pretribulation rapture position mathematically untenable.

context: shall destroy the city and the sanctuary and the end thereof shall be with a flood and unto the end of the war, desolations are determined
point_summary: Daniel 9:26 identifies the Messiah being "cut off" and the subsequent destruction of Jerusalem, establishing the prophetic timeline that frames the yet-unfulfilled 70th week as the coming Tribulation period.

context: he shall confirm the covenant with many for one week
point_summary: Daniel 9:27 identifies the Antichrist's covenant with Israel as the event that initiates the seven-year Tribulation, with his mid-point desecration of the temple marking the abomination of desolation.

context: in the midst of the week, he shall cause the sacrifice and the oblation to cease. And for the overspreading of abominations, he shall make it desolate
point_summary: Daniel 9:27 identifies the Antichrist confirming a covenant that inaugurates the seven-year Tribulation, with his mid-point desecration of sacrifice marking the abomination of desolation.

context: the Rapture occurs before the 70th week of Daniel
point_summary: Daniel 9:27's "70th week" provides the chronological framework for locating the Rapture—whether pre-, mid-, or post-Tribulation—relative to that seven-year period's start, midpoint, and end.

context: the abomination of desolation
point_summary: The covenant that initiates Daniel's 70th week provides a calculable starting point that, under mid- or post-Trib views, would allow believers to predict the Rapture's timing—contradicting the biblical teaching that no man knows the hour.

context: in this passage of scripture in chapter four and chapter five
point_summary: Daniel 7's vision of the Ancient of Days on a fiery throne is cited as Old Testament confirmation of the heavenly throne-room scene John beholds in Revelation 4–5.

context: in this passage of scripture in chapter four and chapter five
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: turn over to Daniel chapter number seven
point_summary: Daniel 7:9–10 is brought alongside Revelation 4–5 to confirm that the Ancient of Days enthroned in Daniel's vision is the same eternal God John beholds in the heavenly throne room.

context: I beheld till the thrones were cast down. The ancient of days did sit, whose garment was white as snow and the hair of his head like the pure wool. His throne was like the fiery flame and his wheels as a burning fire.
point_summary: Daniel 7:9 is read as a parallel vision confirming the throne-room scene in Revelation 4–5, showing the Ancient of Days enthroned in glory with innumerable attendants before the opened books of judgment.

context: A fiery stream issued and came forth from before him. Thousand thousands ministered unto him and 10,000 times 10,000 stood before him. The judgment was set and the books were open.
point_summary: Daniel 7:10's vision of innumerable thousands ministering before God's throne is cited as parallel testimony confirming the heavenly throne-room scene John beholds in Revelation 4.

context: I was reading through the book of Daniel this morning
point_summary: Nebuchadnezzar's boast over Babylon and his subsequent humiliation stand as the biblical precedent that God abases the proud, a warning applied directly to contemporary national leaders.

context: God is able to abase
point_summary: National pride that exalts human achievement above God invites divine humbling, as Nebuchadnezzar's own confession in Daniel 4 demonstrates that God is able to abase the proudest rulers on earth.

context: there are some guys that are gonna go to a fiery furnace, and they made a decision based on principle
point_summary: Decisions grounded in biblical principle—not emotional reaction—endure under pressure, as the three Hebrew men demonstrated by refusing to bow regardless of consequence.
